story:
Cheap beer and regret sting my nose as Gavin pins me against the Kappa bathroom’s marble tiles. His hands fumble at my buttons while I try to force the movie-version of desire to show up. Instead it feels like I’m on his checklist: Hook up with the ethnic girl. Check. Anika’s voice barrels through the door, mortifying and merciful, and when Gavin mutters “Get rid of her” like he’s entitled to my body, the spell snaps. I yank my jeans up. He huffs, “All Desi girls are prudes,” and the slap of it follows me as I walk out and don’t look back.

Anika is my anchor in the whiter-than-snowglobe world of Stonehaven. She rolls her eyes at Gavin, at sorority blondes who veer away like we’re contagious, at the tightrope we’re always walking—too Indian for some spaces, too American for others. Then a voice behind me says I’m right not to belong here. I spin into Krish: tall, infuriatingly gorgeous, undeniably South Asian, the kind of person who looks like he belongs everywhere. He recognizes me from Biology—my heart does an obnoxious little flutter—and assumes I’m rushing Kappa. I bite back. He smirks. I leave. His name echoes anyway.

Greek Row the next morning feels like window shopping for identities. Kappa sells “class values” (code for trust funds), Alpha hawks GPA worship, Delta crafts itself to death. Sigma, though, promises gaming tournaments, movie marathons, zero dress code—no performance of “perfect girl,” no performative anything. Then I spot Krish laughing with Sigma brothers like he owns the air. Of course he does. I whisper to Anika that the only way we’d fit anywhere is if we joined a frat. We look at the wigs and makeup we don’t need. We already dress like armor. The universe drops a Sigma flyer in my hands. Fine. Challenge accepted.

That evening we roll up as “guys”—low voices, wide stances, YouTube-level facial hair—and Sigma lets us in without measuring our edges. I destroy Devonte at Gran Turismo, and he grins before announcing their dumb “tradition”: jump in the pool naked—or find a volunteer. No one is cheap. My blood goes cold. We split to negotiate. Upstairs, a hand yanks me into a linen closet. Krish. He’s seen through everything—my eyes, my posture, the way I ask questions like I’m always proving something. I admit the truth: Sigma feels like breathing. He surprises me back. Being the “perfect Desi boy” is a trap too—too American, too traditional, never enough for somebody. He offers to jump for us if I’ll be his fake girlfriend—to pause parental pressure and friend setups. I set a hard line: no sexual anything. He’s relieved. We almost kiss. Anika barrels in. Ceiling tiles become my religion.

Krish is the one who strips and cannonballs—consenting, laughing, making the cold look like a choice. We pass. As we’re leaving, he catches my arm and says, “So? Fake girlfriend?” Sigma hums around us, a place where skill matters more than packaging. For the first time since arriving, belonging doesn’t feel like a costume. “Yeah,” I tell him, ignoring the fireworks in my ribcage. “I can do fake.”

On the walk back, Anika nudges, all smug. “That chemistry wasn’t fake.” I tug my cap lower and lie badly. In my head, the almost-kiss rewinds, and Krish says my name like it means something. Maybe belonging isn’t about shrinking or translating. Maybe it’s about choosing the people who see you—and letting them see you back.
